title: Classifying Triangles by Sides and Angles

steps.instruction: Count how many sides are equal.
steps.work: All 3 equal → equilateral. Exactly 2 equal → isosceles. No sides equal → scalene.
steps.explanation: The three types based on side lengths are equilateral, isosceles, and scalene.

steps.work: All angles < 90° → acute. One angle = 90° → right. One angle > 90° → obtuse.
steps.explanation: Every triangle fits one of these three angle categories.

steps.instruction: A triangle with sides 3 cm, 4 cm, 5 cm and a 90° angle.
steps.work: Sides: all different → scalene. Angles: has a 90° angle → right. It is a scalene right triangle.
steps.explanation: Triangles have two classifications — one for sides and one for angles.

steps.instruction: How many pairs of parallel sides does the shape have?
steps.work: 0 pairs → irregular quadrilateral. 1 pair → trapezoid. 2 pairs → parallelogram.
steps.explanation: The number of pairs of parallel sides is the first thing to check.

steps.instruction: If it is a parallelogram, check if angles are 90°.
steps.work: 4 right angles → rectangle. Not all right angles → general parallelogram.
steps.explanation: A rectangle is a special parallelogram with all right angles.

steps.instruction: Check if all four sides are equal.
steps.work: 4 equal sides (no right angles) → rhombus. 4 equal sides + 4 right angles → square.
steps.explanation: A square is the most specific quadrilateral — it is a rectangle, a rhombus, and a parallelogram all at once.

steps.work: Square → Rectangle → Parallelogram → Quadrilateral. Square → Rhombus → Parallelogram.
steps.explanation: Each shape down the chain adds fewer requirements. A square has the most properties.
